title: Pyrography shading
subtitle: Building tonal gradients and texture with the shading tip on a wire-nib burner
excerpt: Shading in pyrography builds tonal gradients by varying the speed of the tip across the wood surface: slower movement produces darker marks; faster movement produces lighter ones. A shading tip has a wide, flat or spoon-shaped nib that covers more surface area than a writing tip, allowing large areas to be toned quickly. This technique covers the heat setting drill, the three fundamental stroke types for shading, and the grain-direction rules that keep shading even.
sourceNotes: The Art Amateur vol. 47 (1902) for pyrography shading technique and tip types. Handicraft for Girls (1914) by Idabelle McGlauflin for shading exercises and grain-direction guidance.

content.text: Shading is the technique that moves pyrography from outline work into tonal drawing. With a shading tip on a wire-nib burner set to a medium heat, you can get a full tonal range from near-white to near-black. The shade depends on how slowly or quickly the nib moves across the surface. A wire-nib burner is much better for shading than a solid-tip burner. The wire nib lets you set the heat finely. A solid-tip burner runs at one fixed heat and cannot give the graded tones that make shading work.
What it does

A shading pass tones an area of the wood surface to a consistent or graduated value. Multiple overlapping passes at different speeds build tonal gradients. Dark areas receive slow, overlapping strokes; light areas receive fast, light-pressure strokes.
When to use it

After outlining and detail work, for adding volume and dimension to a burned design. Shading is also used to create textured backgrounds and to define the difference between foreground and background in a composition.
What you need

A wire-nib pyrography burner with temperature control. A shading tip. A cooling stand to rest the hot tip between passes. A wood surface sanded to 400 grit before burning begins; rough wood absorbs heat unevenly and produces patchy shading.
Safety
Work in a ventilated space; burning wood produces fine smoke. Always rest the tip on the cooling stand between passes; a hot tip placed on any surface other than the stand is a burn and fire risk.
Step-by-step
Step 1: Prepare the surface

Sand the wood blank to 400 grit before any burning. Wipe off the dust with a dry cloth. A surface sanded to 400 absorbs heat evenly; a surface at 180 grit or below has visible scratch marks that will show through the shading as lighter streaks.
Step 2: Set the heat and test

Set the burner temperature to a medium setting (typically 5 to 6 on a 1 to 10 scale for sycamore or lime; lower for soft birch, higher for dense oak). Test on a scrap piece of the same wood: a medium-speed stroke should produce a warm mid-brown tone. If the stroke is very pale, increase the heat slightly; if it is dark brown at medium speed, reduce it.
Step 3: The three shading strokes

Three stroke types cover all shading needs. The long flat stroke: move the tip in straight lines across the area in one direction, overlapping each stroke by a third. This produces an even, consistent tone. The circular stroke: move the tip in small overlapping circles across the area; this produces a mottled, softly textured tone. The stipple: press the tip briefly onto the surface in a series of dots; this builds tone without directional marks, useful for very fine gradients near a highlight area.
Step 4: Build the gradient

Start with the darkest area and work toward the lightest. Apply the first shading passes at slow speed in the dark zones. Progressively increase speed as the strokes approach the light zones. Overlap the border between dark and light zones with several transitional passes at a speed midway between the two extremes. The transition from dark to light should be gradual across at least 10 mm; an abrupt boundary looks unnatural.
What it looks like when it is right
A smooth gradient from dark to light with no visible stroke boundaries in the transition zone. Dark areas are a consistent warm brown; highlights are the bare wood colour. No patches or streaks.
Common mistakes
Dark patches in the gradient: the tip paused at a single point. Never let the tip rest in contact with the surface; keep it moving at all times.
Visible stroke lines rather than smooth tone: the overlap between strokes was too small. Overlap each stroke by at least a third of the nib width.

The grain shows through the shading unevenly: the surface was not sanded finely enough before burning, or the wood grain is very coarse (oak, ash). Use lime or sycamore for the smoothest shading results.
